metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the allowlist-absence verify command to cover both workflows: `! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml && ! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' .github/workflows/test.yml`
evidence: AC text: `! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` — no parallel check for `test.yml`
Plan step 2d: 'The step no longer reads `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt`' applies to BOTH workflows, but only one is verified by the AC
findings.finding: AC2's allowlist-absence check (`! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist'`) only verifies `gerrit-verify.yaml`; `test.yml` is not checked, so the new gate in `test.yml` could still read the old allowlist and the AC would silently pass.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — second AC bullet (`! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml`)
scenarios: An implementer rewrites gerrit-verify.yaml correctly but forgets to remove the allowlist read from test.yml; the AC passes with no error.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the single-file grep with: `! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml && ! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' .github/workflows/test.yml`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies the missing-file guard: e.g., `LIMIT=''; [ -z "$LIMIT" ] && echo 'limit file missing' && exit 1` — confirm exit 1 when the file is absent.
evidence: Step 2c: 'Also fail if the limit file is MISSING in the change (guards deletion of the lock).'
No AC bullet tests the behavior when `.github/module-size-limit.txt` is absent from the working tree.
findings.finding: Step 2c specifies a 'fail if the limit file is MISSING in the change' behavior (guarding deletion of the lock), but no acceptance criterion verifies this missing-file path fires correctly.
findings.location: What / step 2c ('Also fail if the limit file is MISSING in the change (guards deletion of the lock).')
scenarios: An implementer forgets to add the missing-file guard; Phase 2b accidentally deletes the limit file and the gate silently passes.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'Simulate missing limit file: unset LIMIT (or set it to empty); run the gate snippet; assert exit 1 with an appropriate error message.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC verifying that the gate exits 1 when .github/module-size-limit.txt is absent (e.g., run the gate snippet with the file removed and assert exit 1 with the override message).
evidence: What §2c states: 'Also fail if the limit file is MISSING in the change (guards deletion of the lock)'
No AC references this behavior or provides a verification command for it
findings.finding: The 'missing limit file guard' behavior (gate fails if .github/module-size-limit.txt is absent from the change) is introduced in What §2c but has no corresponding acceptance criterion.
findings.location: What — §2c ('Also fail if the limit file is MISSING in the change (guards deletion of the lock)') / Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: A contributor deletes module-size-limit.txt thinking it's safe — the gate should catch it, but this path is never verified in the ACs, so a mis-implementation (e.g., the check is wrong or omitted) would go undetected

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies both `module-size-allowlist.txt` and `module-size-ceilings.txt` still exist (e.g., `test -f .github/module-size-allowlist.txt && test -f .github/module-size-ceilings.txt`).
evidence: What item 5: 'KEEP the (now-empty) `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` and `.github/module-size-ceilings.txt` files in this change (Phase 2b deletes them). This keeps main's current gate green on THIS change's CI.'
No AC contains a verify step such as `test -f .github/module-size-allowlist.txt && test -f .github/module-size-ceilings.txt`.
findings.finding: What item 5 describes a preservation deliverable — 'KEEP the (now-empty) `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` and `.github/module-size-ceilings.txt` files in this change' — but no AC verifies that these files still exist after the change lands. This matters because their presence is load-bearing for Phase 2a's CI green-pass on main.
scenarios: Implementer accidentally deletes the files (e.g., IDE bulk cleanup), all other ACs pass, but main's current gate breaks on this change's CI — exactly the failure the plan warns against.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: `- [ ] Legacy allowlist/ceilings files are preserved (verify: \`test -f .github/module-size-allowlist.txt && test -f .github/module-size-ceilings.txt\`)`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC bullet 2's verify step to also assert `! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' .github/workflows/test.yml`, covering both workflows as described in What item 2d.
evidence: AC bullet 2 verify: '`! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml`'

What item 2d: 'The step no longer reads `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` or `.github/module-size-ceilings.txt`' (applies to BOTH workflows per the What preamble: 'Rewrite the Module-size gate step in BOTH')
findings.finding: AC bullet 2's verify step checks only `gerrit-verify.yaml` for absence of allowlist reads (`! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml`), but What item 2d says BOTH workflows 'no longer read' those files. The equivalent check against `test.yml` is absent, leaving described removal work in test.yml uncovered by any criterion.
scenarios: Implementer removes allowlist read from gerrit-verify.yaml but forgets test.yml; AC 2 still passes green, silently leaving the old read in test.yml.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC bullet 2: `&& ! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' .github/workflows/test.yml` to the verify command.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a verify step to AC bullet 4 (or a new AC) confirming the old allowlist/ceilings assertions are absent, e.g., `! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py`.
evidence: What item 3: 'Replace `tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py`: drop the allowlist/ceilings sync assertions'
AC bullet 4: 'verify: `pytest tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py -q`' — only checks exit 0, not assertion content.
T4 migration note: criteria should verify BOTH removal and replacement.
findings.finding: What item 3 says to 'drop the allowlist/ceilings sync assertions' from `test_module_size_contract.py`, but AC bullet 4 only verifies that the test *passes* (`pytest … -q`). Since the legacy files are kept (non-empty content is irrelevant but they exist), old sync assertions might still pass if the files are present — a passing run does not confirm the old assertions were removed.
scenarios: Implementer adds new assertions but forgets to delete old ones; pytest passes (old files still present); the contract test is still coupled to legacy files that Phase 2b will delete, creating a latent failure.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC bullet 4's verify: `&& ! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist\|module-size-ceilings' tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify or specify the `origin` remote configuration produced by `checkout-gerrit-change-action` in `gerrit-verify.yaml`, and if `origin` does not resolve to GitHub's `main`, replace `git fetch -q origin main` with `git fetch -q https://github.com/${{ github.repository }} main` (matching the pattern already used in `require-ticket` and `verify-identity` steps in that workflow).
evidence: Plan text: 'git fetch -q origin main; BASE=$(git show origin/main:.github/module-size-limit.txt 2>/dev/null || echo ''')'
gerrit-verify.yaml require-ticket step: 'git fetch --depth=1 "https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}" "+tickets:refs/remotes/gh-tickets"' — uses explicit GitHub URL, not `origin`
gerrit-verify.yaml verify-identity step: 'git fetch "https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}" ...' — same pattern, avoiding `origin`
release.yml uses 'git fetch --no-tags origin main' ONLY after actions/checkout@v7 (GitHub-backed remote), not after checkout-gerrit-change-action
Partially-verified: checkout-gerrit-change-action remote configuration is unconfirmed from plan text alone
findings.finding: The limit-lock snippet (`git fetch -q origin main` then `git show origin/main:.github/module-size-limit.txt`) assumes `origin` points to the GitHub repo where `main` lives, but in `gerrit-verify.yaml` the `checkout-gerrit-change-action` may configure `origin` to the Gerrit server/mirror rather than the GitHub remote. The existing `require-ticket` and `verify-identity` steps in `gerrit-verify.yaml` explicitly fetch via `https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}` instead of `origin main`, which is a strong signal that `origin` in that context does not reliably resolve to GitHub's `main`. If `git fetch -q origin main` fails silently (exit 0) or fetches a Gerrit ref without the limit file, `$BASE` becomes empty and the lock never fires — meaning the lock is inoperative in the Gerrit-verified lane.
findings.location: What / item 2c — limit-lock shell snippet; gerrit-verify.yaml context
scenarios: The `gerrit-verify.yaml` build-and-test job uses `checkout-gerrit-change-action`; if `origin` there points to the Gerrit server, `git fetch -q origin main` either fails or returns a Gerrit ref without a `main` branch, making `$BASE` empty — the lock check silently passes every time, defeating the admin-lock purpose in the Verified gate.

A contributor edits `.github/module-size-limit.txt`, the lock fires in `test.yml` (post-merge branch CI via actions/checkout where origin=GitHub), but passes in `gerrit-verify.yaml` (pre-merge) — the change lands with a Verified +1 despite the locked limit being changed.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `git fetch -q origin main` with `git fetch -q "https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}" main` and adjust `git show` to `git show FETCH_HEAD:.github/module-size-limit.txt` (or keep the `origin/main` ref after the fetch sets it). This mirrors the proven pattern from `require-ticket`/`verify-identity` in `gerrit-verify.yaml` and is safe in `test.yml` too since `${{ github.repository }}` is always available.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that runs the shell gate's cap-enforcement snippet directly against a crafted tree (e.g., a temp file with >800 lines) and asserts exit 1, and against a file with <=800 lines and asserts exit 0, to prove the cutover end-to-end.
evidence: AC2 proves the limit file is referenced in the YAML (grep count) but does not run the shell logic.
AC4 (`pytest tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py`) exercises a Python mirror of the cap logic, not the shell step itself.
AC6 exercises only the lock snippet (BASE vs LIMIT comparison), not the cap-enforcement snippet (wc -l > LIMIT).
Notes: 'E5: the gate is shell (oracle = running it against crafted trees, per the ACs)' — but no such crafted-tree AC exists.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion exercises the shell gate's absolute-cap enforcement end-to-end (running the actual shell step against a tree that contains an over-cap file). The Notes section acknowledges 'E5: the gate is shell (oracle = running it against crafted trees, per the ACs)' but the ACs only grep for strings in the workflow files or run Python tests — neither actually executes the shell gate against a crafted tree.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — all AC bullets; Notes section ('E5: the gate is shell (oracle = running it against crafted trees, per the ACs)')
scenarios: The shell gate is mis-authored (e.g., `wc -l` counts blank lines differently, or the glob pattern is wrong) but all ACs pass because none run the shell; an over-cap file slips through CI.
The `src/rebar/**/*.py` glob in the shell step is accidentally narrowed (e.g. missing `**`) and only checks top-level files; the Python contract test still passes.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'Simulate cap enforcement: create a temp file of 801 lines; run the gate snippet `LIMIT=800; wc -l <file> | awk ...`; assert exit 1. Create a file of 800 lines; assert exit 0.' This can be a small shell script or an additional pytest parametrized case in the contract test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that runs the gate script against a crafted tree containing a file with exactly $LIMIT+1 lines and asserts exit code 1, and one at exactly $LIMIT lines asserting exit 0 — fulfilling the 'crafted trees' oracle the Notes already promise.
evidence: 'E5: the gate is shell (oracle = running it against crafted trees, per the ACs)' — but no AC creates a >800-line file and runs the gate script against it
AC2 verifies structure ('grep -c module-size-limit.txt') not behavior
AC4 runs pytest on the real src/rebar tree, which is already clean — it cannot exercise the over-cap branch of the gate
findings.finding: The absolute-cap failure path of the new gate script (a file with wc -l > $LIMIT causing an exit 1) has no behavioral acceptance criterion that actually runs the gate against a crafted over-cap tree. The Notes state the oracle is 'running it against crafted trees, per the ACs' but no AC exercises this path; AC2 only greps for the limit-file reference structurally, and AC4 tests the contract on the real codebase (all files already under cap).
scenarios: The gate script mis-implements the wc -l comparison (e.g., uses >= vs >, or counts blank lines differently) — no AC would catch this because no AC runs the gate against an over-cap file
The $LIMIT variable is mis-read (e.g., trailing newline in cat output) causing the numeric comparison to silently pass — not caught by any behavioral AC
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: 'The gate rejects an over-cap file: create a temp file with 801 lines under src/rebar/, run the gate step (or the relevant shell snippet), assert exit 1; a 800-line file passes (exit 0). Verify: `python -c "open('/tmp/src/rebar/big.py','w').write('x\n'*801)"` then run the cap-check snippet.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the parent epic (716f) to reflect the 2a/2b split before merging this leaf — add a Phase 2b entry to the parent's Decomposition and confirm AC2/AC3 are satisfied only when BOTH 2a and 2b land — so the leaf is a provable subset of an updated parent contract rather than a divergence from the current one.
evidence: Parent Decomposition: 'Phase 2 — one child story: delete the allowlist+ceilings mechanism, replace the contract test with an absolute 'nothing over 800' test, prune the architecture.md offender table, and add the limit-lock gate.'
Parent AC2: 'The allowlist/ceilings mechanism (both files + the sync contract test + the shell gate's allowlist branch) is removed; CI still fails any new over-cap file'
Leaf What §5: 'KEEP the (now-empty) .github/module-size-allowlist.txt and .github/module-size-ceilings.txt files in this change (Phase 2b deletes them).'
Leaf Notes: 'Phase 2b (follow-up) deletes the orphaned allowlist/ceilings files, prunes the docs/architecture.md offenders table, and updates AGENTS.md — kept separate'
findings.finding: The leaf splits the parent's 'Phase 2 — one child story' into Phase 2a + Phase 2b without updating the parent, and explicitly keeps files the parent AC2 says must be removed (allowlist/ceilings), contradicting the parent's stated decomposition and acceptance criteria.
findings.location: Leaf What §5 / Notes; Parent Decomposition 'Phase 2' and Parent AC2
scenarios: After Phase 2a lands, parent AC2 ('mechanism is removed') remains unmet — the two allowlist/ceilings files still exist, creating a state where the epic's own acceptance criteria are false.
Phase 2b (a second child story) is implied by the leaf but not registered in the parent's decomposition — reviewers of the epic cannot see that Phase 2 is incomplete.
If Phase 2b is never created or is blocked, the parent epic lands with its AC2 permanently unmet.
findings.suggested_fix: In the parent epic (716f), revise the Decomposition to read: 'Phase 2a — swap the gate to absolute cap + limit-lock (keeps legacy files for CI ordering); Phase 2b — delete allowlist/ceilings files, prune architecture.md offender table, update AGENTS.md.' Update AC2 to reference Phase 2b as the completion point. Then re-review the parent, after which this leaf is a clean subset.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
verify command `wc -l` inspects output with `wc` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable

verify command `grep -q 'must override' .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml && grep -q 'must override' .github/workflows/test.yml` greps the unanchored word 'must' — it substring-matches (e.g. 'review_must'); anchor it (\b, ^$, or a quoted key like '"must":')
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add minimal documentation of the limit-lock mechanism to this leaf (e.g. a brief AGENTS.md note), OR explicitly include documentation as a Phase 2b deliverable in the parent epic's updated AC3, so the 'documented' requirement is provably covered.
evidence: Parent AC3: 'A hard gate blocks changing the 800 limit through the normal contribution process (admin manual override required); documented'
Leaf Notes: 'Phase 2b (follow-up) deletes the orphaned allowlist/ceilings files, prunes the docs/architecture.md offenders table, and updates AGENTS.md — kept separate'
findings.finding: The leaf defers documentation of the limit-lock gate (AGENTS.md, docs/architecture.md) to Phase 2b, but the parent AC3 requires the hard gate to be 'documented' as part of Phase 2's delivery — the leaf contradicts this criterion.
scenarios: After Phase 2a lands, the new limit-lock gate is live but undocumented in AGENTS.md or architecture.md — contributors have no canonical reference explaining why their limit change was blocked or how to get an admin override.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback / back-out note acknowledging that reverting Phase 2a requires an admin force-submit (due to the missing-file guard) and document the procedure, satisfying T4's reversibility requirement.
evidence: What item 2c: 'Also fail if the limit file is MISSING in the change (guards deletion of the lock).'
No rollback path or back-out procedure is mentioned anywhere in the plan.
Notes section describes Phase 2b as a follow-up but gives no revert guidance for Phase 2a itself.
findings.finding: Once Phase 2a lands on main, the missing-file guard ('Also fail if the limit file is MISSING in the change — guards deletion of the lock') makes a full rollback of Phase 2a self-blocking: a revert PR that removes `.github/module-size-limit.txt` would be rejected by the very gate it is trying to revert, requiring admin force-submit. The plan does not acknowledge this irreversibility or provide a rollback path.
scenarios: A post-landing regression is found; the team tries to revert Phase 2a via a normal PR; the gate on main blocks the revert because the limit file is absent from the revert's tree; recovery requires an admin force-submit that may not be immediately available.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection to Notes: 'To revert Phase 2a after it lands on main, an admin must force-submit the revert PR past the missing-file guard. Steps: (1) prepare a revert branch; (2) admin applies force-submit label; (3) verify gate is gone from main before proceeding with any 2b work.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AGENTS.md section (or ADR) in Phase 2a describing the admin-locked limit pattern, the force-submit override mechanism, and the rationale for CI-gate-in-code over Gerrit ACL; scope the Phase 2b AGENTS.md note to cover only offenders-table/allowlist cleanup, not the new pattern introduction.
evidence: 'Phase 2b ... updates AGENTS.md — kept separate because the gate must stop reading those files (this change) BEFORE they can be deleted'
The stated reason for deferral covers file deletion ordering only; it does not apply to adding new content documenting the locked-limit pattern to AGENTS.md

T5e(c): 'A novel architectural decision is captured in an ADR / AGENTS.md / design-doc update' — the admin-locked limit / force-submit override policy is a novel governance decision not documented in this change
ABSENCE: no AGENTS.md update is listed in the ## What items
findings.finding: Phase 2a defers all documentation updates (AGENTS.md, docs/architecture.md) to Phase 2b, leaving both docs stale after 2a lands. The deferral is justified for file deletions (CI ordering), but updating AGENTS.md to describe the new admin-locked limit pattern and the 'no Gerrit ACL / force-submit override' policy has no such ordering constraint — it can and should land with the gate rewrite.
findings.location: ## Notes (Phase 2b description) and ## Why ('Per the operator decision, this is a CI-gate-in-code mechanism (no Gerrit ACL)')
scenarios: A contributor reads AGENTS.md after Phase 2a lands and sees the old allowlist/escape-hatch model described, with no mention of the locked-limit or force-submit-override requirement — they misunderstand the enforcement model.
Phase 2b is delayed or abandoned; the architectural decision about CI-gate-in-code vs Gerrit ACL is never captured in a durable doc.
findings.suggested_fix: In ## What, add item 6: 'Update AGENTS.md: document the new locked-limit contract (single-source `.github/module-size-limit.txt`, absolute cap, limit-lock behavior, and that changing the limit requires an admin force-submit). Do NOT defer this to 2b — file-deletion ordering does not apply to new content additions.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit error handling for `git fetch -q origin main` failure (e.g., guard with `|| { echo '::error::git fetch failed; cannot verify limit lock'; exit 1; }`) and document the shallow-clone prerequisite (`fetch-depth: 0` or equivalent) so the lock cannot be silently bypassed on a network or depth error.
evidence: Plan text: "`git fetch -q origin main`; `BASE=$(git show origin/main:.github/module-size-limit.txt 2>/dev/null || echo '')`"
ABSENCE: no `|| true`, retry, or explicit handling of `git fetch` non-zero exit
ABSENCE: no note on behavior under CI network isolation or shallow-clone environments
If fetch fails and BASE='' the lock condition `[ -n "$BASE" ] && [ "$BASE" != "$LIMIT" ]` evaluates false, silently passing a limit change
findings.finding: The `git fetch -q origin main` in the limit-lock step is an external network call with no error handling. If the fetch silently fails (network blip, credential issue), `git show origin/main:.github/module-size-limit.txt` may return the value from stale local refs OR fail — but because `|| echo ''` is only on the `git show` line, not on the fetch, the net result is that BASE may be set to empty string (lock skipped, illicit limit change slips through) or the step hard-fails on a transient network issue, blocking unrelated changes. Neither outcome is specified or mitigated.
scenarios: Transient network failure during CI causes all PRs to fail the gate with a confusing error unrelated to module size
Shallow-clone CI environment (common in GitHub Actions with `fetch-depth: 0` not set) causes git show to fail, BASE='', lock bypassed silently
A contributor edits module-size-limit.txt; network hiccup during their CI run; lock check skipped; change lands without admin force-submit
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the fetch+show block with:
```sh
git fetch -q origin main || { echo '::error::Could not fetch origin/main to verify limit lock. Ensure fetch-depth is 0 and network is available.'; exit 1; }
BASE=$(git show origin/main:.github/module-size-limit.txt 2>/dev/null || echo '')
```
Also add a note in the plan (and workflow comments) that `actions/checkout` must use `fetch-depth: 0` for this step to work correctly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief rationale (1–2 sentences in the Why or Notes section) explaining why the established alternatives (Gerrit submit requirements / CODEOWNERS required-review on the limit file) were not used, so the bespoke in-CI limit-lock is justified as a novel pattern rather than an oversight.
evidence: Plan states 'Per the operator decision, this is a CI-gate-in-code mechanism (no Gerrit ACL)' — operator decision acknowledged but not explained.
The limit-lock pattern (git show origin/main:<file> compared in-CI) does not appear anywhere in the existing workflow files, making it novel to this codebase.

Established alternatives: Gerrit submit requirements (require a +2 from a specific group), GitHub CODEOWNERS + required review on .github/module-size-limit.txt, Gerrit's Label configuration with exclusive permissions.
ABSENCE: No rationale given for why those alternatives were ruled out (e.g. Gerrit ACL not configured, CODEOWNERS not enforced on the mirror, etc.).
findings.finding: The 'limit-lock' mechanism (comparing origin/main's limit file against the working-tree value inside CI, failing the gate if they differ) is a novel architectural pattern for an admin-only change veto. Established alternatives exist — Gerrit submit requirements/labels that could require a specific score from a privileged reviewer, GitHub CODEOWNERS with required-review on the limit file, or a Gerrit pre-receive hook — but the plan only notes 'per the operator decision, this is a CI-gate-in-code mechanism (no Gerrit ACL)' without articulating why those alternatives are inadequate for this repo's setup.
findings.location: Why section: 'Per the operator decision, this is a CI-gate-in-code mechanism (no Gerrit ACL).' / What item 2c (LIMIT LOCK logic)
scenarios: A reviewer or future maintainer cannot assess whether the bespoke in-CI veto is the right trade-off or whether a Gerrit label-based approach would have been simpler and less fragile (e.g., less sensitive to shallow-clone/fetch-availability issues).
If the operator later configures proper Gerrit submit requirements, the in-CI veto becomes redundant dead code with no documented rationale for removal.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
